title: Introduction and Research Context
section_goal: Introduce the research domain of pedestrian crossing behavior, establish the importance of understanding both intentional violations and distraction effects, and outline the scope of this review. Use Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses), Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions as the organizing lens across 6 matrix rows.

blocks.purpose: Frame Introduction and Research Context around Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses), Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions and clarify why this evidence cluster matters.

theme_refs.synthesis_note: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), and extending it by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions

theme_refs.label: Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested

When descriptive norm was added to the model, subjective norm became insignificant while descriptive norm emerged as a stronger predictor, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family and friends' behavior had greater influence than perceived approval from them

title: Theoretical Foundations of Pedestrian Crossing Behavior
section_goal: Present the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework as applied to pedestrian violating behavior, and establish the psychological constructs (attitudes, norms, perceived control) that inform behavioral prediction models. Use Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions, Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses) as the organizing lens across 6 matrix rows.

title: Mobile Phone Distraction Effects on Crossing Performance
section_goal: Synthesize experimental findings on how different types of mobile phone use (texting, conversation, music) differentially impair pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation. Use Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses), Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions as the organizing lens across 6 matrix rows.

blocks.purpose: Frame Mobile Phone Distraction Effects on Crossing Performance around Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses), Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions and clarify why this evidence cluster matters.

theme_refs.label: Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect the behavior of pedestrians while crossing the street at a signalized intersection, addressing the growing safety concern of mobile phone use among pedestrians.

sentence_plan.directive: Introduce the subsection focus and explain why this evidence cluster matters for the review argument.
Anchor the framing in the strongest relevant evidence, themes, or matrix signals rather than generic setup.
Close with a sentence that narrows toward the next evidence-bearing claim.

blocks.intended_purpose: Anchor the section in 6 extracted claim signal(s) and the strongest matrix evidence.

theme_refs.label: Survey Data From Dalian, China (260 Complete And Valid Responses)
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Survey data from Dalian, China (260 complete and valid responses) appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

theme_refs.label: Predicting Pedestrians' Violating Road Crossing Behavior Intentions
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrians' crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly affecting all three crossing behavior variables and five visual attention variables

Music distraction increases pedestrian pupil diameter and reduces scanning frequency as well as fixation points and fixation times toward traffic signal area priority, indicating cognitive load without extreme behavioral impairment
